The O'-Word Contest
by Reddick and Campbell

This puzzle contains many familiar words, abbreviations, or combinations that are well-known to orienteers. See how good you are at finding them! You may read any one direction you like, but there are no wrap-arounds. There is no time limit, but if you find more than 40 in less than an hour, you qualify for the U.S. O-Word Team Bullpen. More than 50 makes you a first string U.S. O-Word Team member, and 57 qualifies you as WORLD ELITE CLASS! But if you find more than 57, and justify them all as orienteering-related, send your list to WSOA, P.O. Box 1977, Tacoma, WA 98411-1977. The entry with the greatest number of words will receive a valuable orienteering prize. Judges' arbitrary decisions will be final. Look for the answers and winner's name in a forthcoming issue.

Club Meeting September 19

Next Issue:

  • Centennial Games/State Championships results
  • Western States results
  • British Columbia Championships results
  • Schedule of Events
  • Cascade Chatter

The September club meeting will feature another installment of "Course Setting For Beginners" as well as training tips and a review of the Western States Championships on Orcas Island. Don't miss it, especially the post-meeting get-together at Donna's Ice Cream Parlor in Roosevelt Square.

Page 2

Western States Championships Information

If you are attending the Western States Championships September 9-10, please read the following for information about the weekend.

Camp Orkila
If you are staying at Camp Orkila, please bring:
sleeping bag
pillow
towel
flashlight
personal gear.

Please do NOT bring:
pets
alcohol.

Transportation
For those staying at Camp Orkila or West Beach Resort, buses will pick up at the Orcas ferry landing on Friday, September 8th at:
5:30, 8:50, and 10:05 p.m.

Check-in
Check-in for pre-registered participants will be at Camp Orkila at the following times:
Friday 1-10:30 p.m.
Saturday 7- 10 a.m.

Parking At the Meet Site
No parking is available at the meet site. However, plenty of free parking is available at Camp Orkila, and we will have buses to take you from there to the meet. If you are not staying at Camp Orkila, transportation to and from the meet site is $2 per/person each day.

Allow Time to Get to the Start
Remember that all start times for pre-registered participants have already been assigned, so be sure to allow enough time to get to the start.
The bus ride from Camp Orkila is 20 minutes, loading and unloading 10 minutes, and the walk to the start 50- 75 minutes, plus whatever personal time that you will need. This may sound intimidating, but it really is a nice walk.

For additional information call:
Sandy Kish Miller (206)784-5939 days
Debbie Newell (206)488-7471 evenings
Katie Sprugel (206)365-8742

Kayaking opportunities on Friday are available with Black Fish Paddlers at noon and 3 p.m. (206)376-4041

See you on the island!

Page 3

Cascade Chatter

compiled by Harvey Friedman

As usual, we'll start with positive roster changes as of 30 August 1989. Words of both thanks and welcome go to the following new or renewing club members: Jean Davis, Thomas DeBuys, Jed Dixon, Jeff Hamm family, Peter & Mary Holmberg, Li-Chuan Huang, Kraft family, Katie & Doug Sprugel, Robert Stephens, Ross Stevens, Tim & Wendy Vargo, Susan St. John, Dale Width family.
If you all don't come to meets, you'll probably not see your name in the newsletter until the next time you renew; also, membership cards are only available in person at a club function such as regular meet, meeting, pot-O-luck, but not at any ski-O meets, nor night-O meets, nor canoe-O meets (unless you are lucky), nor training events, nor workshops. If you want to get your handsome membership card, contact the membership coordinator, me (Harvey), at a Cascade sponsored meet or at one of the above-mentioned happenings. I do not have blank membership cards with me when I attend other clubs' functions.
Now on to the fun stuff. Tim Vargo no longer is an individual member but now has his wife Wendy orienteering too....Dale & Nancy Width have a new addition to their family; Quinn was born on 9 July 1989....That is all the local stuff that anyone wrote for me but since Anne and I did attend the World Championships in Orienteering in Sweden this August, I can write a bit about former Cascade members we met there or in Norway....Pam & Sture Carlsson are living in Hedemora where he is director of product development for a forest products company that supplies gear to backcountry users and she is a nurse on "night patrol." Night patrol checks on old people living alone (or together) and handle the usual emergency nursing duties. Though they live only a 5 hour drive from the site of the World Championships, their club and another scheduled a meet the day after the individual championships and at the same time as the "spectators competion" on that map and during the relay championships. 10,000 people attended the WC and the Hedemora group had 4,000 preregistered. People in Sweden enjoy being in the woods and many videotaped the relay since it was shown on national TV....While at the WC (or VM for världsmästerskapen as it is known in Scandinavia), I also met briefly with Göran Larsson who helped get Cascade growing in 1980-1982. His son Robert, who many remember that Göran and Berith either took turns doing one of the easy courses with or ran with Robert on his back, is now 11. Göran wants to come out for PNWOF next year but is still uncertain about whether he will be able to. Anyone trying to phone him should write first, because though he lives in Norberg, only about 20 miles from Hedemora, we were unable to make contact by phone. Wearing my Cascade t-shirt helped me stand out (even more than usual) among the thousands at the championships so that Göran could approach me....Another one who is difficult to contact is Stein Sandven, who did the initial mapping for the Foster Island map. Stein is one of the leading scientists of the Nansen Ocean and Remote Sensing Center, a non-profit institute affiliated with the University of Bergen, and puts in long hours there when he is not away on a cruise or on the Arctic ice. He no longer has the time to be active on the organizing or mapping side of things, but does run in meets when he can. He jogs in the rain (Bergen has more rain than Seattle) on the steep hillsides around Bergen to stay fit. Though his club is one of the 5 cooperating to put on the World Cup in Norway near Voss next June, Stein is just too busy to get involved, especially since they have many enthusiastic volunteers. If any friends of Stein do find themselves in Bergen and want to contact him, the best thing to do is to phone him at work....We spent a day with Ingemar Jansson (who did the U. W., Foster Island, and much of the Lake Tradition maps in 1983-84), his wife, and 16 month old son in Oslo. Sindre (meaning "The First" in old Norwegian) is already quite coordinated as is fitting for the offspring of 2 top orienteers. Ingemar married Helga Haverstad, an elite Norwegian orienteer, who has been in the top 10 in Norway for about 10 years. Helga comes from a farm in the Lier valley between Oslo and Drammen and has as much success finding mushrooms and berries as she does orienteering. Ingemar and Helga are building a house in the Lier valley closer to her roots than Oslo is. It is in a new development, has a wonderful view, lots of rooms for guests, should be finished enough to move into by Christmas, and has lots of amanita muscaria mushrooms in the backyard. They will have to get rid of those before the children are tempted each year. Anyway, Ingemar welcomes any Cascade members who remember him to stop and visit when they are in Norway. Even for the new members who never met him, if you wear a Cascade O. C. t-shirt, no doubt you will be just as welcome....For those of you who are wondering if I ever would say who won the World Championship in 1989, yes, I will. Petter Thoresen of Norway had a fantastic run to finish 3 minutes ahead of Kent Olsson of Sweden and fellow Norwegian Håvard Tveite. Marita Skogum of Sweden was 1 minute faster than the rest of the women's field though 2nd and 3rd went to Jana Galikova of Czechoslovakia and to Alida Abola of the Soviet Union. In the relays, it was the men of Norway and the women of Sweden winning. The men had a 3 minute margin of victory but the Swedish women were only 15 seconds ahead of the Czechs. I have maps from the event if anyone is interested....If you want more news about people in the club, you'll have to tell me something or preferably write or scribble me something that is for this column since I have such a bad memory.

For the latest meet information, call the ORIENTEERING HOTLINE at 783-3866

Event Key
A - major event on good quality color map, 5 courses minimum
B - major local event, 3-4 courses minimum
C - local club event, 3 or 4 courses
S - special event (bicycle, canoe, ski, night, etc.)
W - Washington Interscholastic O' League meet (everyone welcome)




Club Key
COC - Cascade O Club; EOC - Ellensburg O Club; EWOC - Eastern Washington O Club; NISQ - Nisqually Orienteers; SAMM - Sammamish O Club; WSOA - Washington State O Assoc.

"BEARING 315" PUBLICATION SCHEDULE

"Bearing 315" is a bi-monthly publication of the Cascade Orienteering Club. You are encouraged to submit orienteering-related material for publication. Send items for publication to the address below by the scheduled deadline. Material received after the deadline may not appear in the next newsletter issue.

The O'-Word Contest by Reddick and Campbell

In the relays, it was the men of Norway and the women of Sweden winning. The men had a 3 minute margin of victory but the Swedish women were only 15 seconds ahead of the Czechs. I have maps from the event if anyone is interested....If you want more news about people in the club, you'll have to tell me something or preferably write or scribble me something that is for this column since I have such a bad memory.
